narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 54.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, LSAT score of 35, middle family income bracket, ranking in the 2nd decile for Year 1 grades and 1st decile for Year 3 grades at your school, and being a white male planning full-time studies. Compared to typical students in our dataset—who average a 3.21 undergrad GPA, 36.7 LSAT, and higher decile rankings around the 5th or 6th for both early years—your academic standings in law school years stand out as notably lower than average. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 1 decile ranking, which had the strongest push toward predicting bar failure, followed closely by your Year 3 decile. Your LSAT score came next in importance, also nudging the prediction toward failure, with your undergraduate GPA and family income playing supporting roles in that direction. Less influential but still contributing were your racial background, gender, and full-time status, all of which aligned with common profiles but didn't counterbalance the academic concerns. In essence, your lower-than-average grades in the early and later years of law school were the top factors signaling higher risk to the model, more so than your test scores or background. While demographics like being a white male full-time student are typical and didn't help or hurt much, they couldn't offset the grade deciles' dominant impact. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven assessment showing how your profile compares to those who historically pass the bar first time. To improve your future chances, focus on actionable steps like targeted study strategies to boost performance in remaining law school courses, perhaps seeking tutoring or academic support to climb those decile rankings. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also strengthen your profile if you reapply elsewhere. Finally, building rigorous bar exam preparation habits early—through practice tests and review courses—can help overcome these predictions and demonstrate your readiness. We believe in second chances, and addressing these academic areas head-on could shift the odds in your favor next time.
